G9C4M1A2 High and Low Chances.pptx
HIGH Chance or LOW Chance?
Grade 9 · Probability · Learn
Needs rework
72.4
Overall score
55%
Mastery coverage
Addressing the missing Notice and Articulation stages (two new screens with contrastive pair reveal and sentence-completion panel), removing the pre-revealed answer from slide 7, and adding persistent reasoning prompts to all independent frames will bring mastery coverage to ~85% and resolve both hard gate failures.
Context Grade 9 · Probability · Kurikulum Merdeka · ID
This consolidation applet delivers a functional drag-to-sort mechanic with culturally authentic Indonesian event cards and frequency-anchored feedback, but falls short of the full six-stage Learn-column arc. Three critical stages are missing (Notice, Articulation, and a graduated Apply), and two hard gates failed (G6: screen content not self-sufficient; G7: screen zones incomplete). The applet addresses M280 and M281 partially but leaves M283 unconfirmed in the storyboard. Addressing the missing Notice and Articulation stages will bring mastery coverage from ~55% to ~85%.
KB evidence: thin — Merged KB evidence — overall: thin
Weak tables: convention_differences, learning_progressions, curricula
Two hard gates failed: G6 (screen content self-sufficient) — slides 7, 9, and 11 lack sufficient scaffolding text or reasoning prompts to carry the interaction without external guidance; G7 (screen zones present) — slides 9 and 11 are missing the three-zone structure (action instruction + scaffolding text + dev notes). All other hard gates (G1, G3, G4, G5, G8) passed. Advisory gate G2 (six-stage progression) is traceable but incomplete.

Vocabulary Registry 2 terms · 1 delivered · 1 gap
Every term this module is responsible for introducing. Source = where the term came from; Status = whether the scorer found it actually introduced.
TermDefinition · observed useSourceStatus
chanceA word describing how likely or unlikely something is to happen, ranging from impossible to certain.
observed: drag uncertain event cards into a HIGH Chance or LOW Chance zone using frequency reasoning as the basis for their judgment
A1 planestablished
uncertain outcomeA result that cannot be predicted with complete confidence before the event occurs.
observed: GAP: use 'uncertain outcome' as a compound term
A1 plannot introduced
What's Working Well 8 criteria at max
Positive observations surfaced from scorer reasoning — criteria that scored at max, grouped by quadrant.
P — Pedagogy · 2
P1 LO alignment & scope 3/3
The LO is discrete (binary sort of uncertain events), maps directly to the module LO, and the applet introduces no out-of-scope content. Full marks awarded.
P7 Inverse operations 3/3
Inverse operations as a criterion applies where conceptually central (addition/subtraction, multiplication/division). For a binary sort applet, the bidirectional classification (HIGH and LOW) is the full scope, and both directions are present. Full marks awarded — no deduction warranted for a criterion that is not conceptually central to this LO type.
D — Design · 1
D6 Pacing 3/3
All screens are student-paced with explicit action gates. Error correction enforces re-engagement. Completion screen allows review. Full marks.
X — Experience · 2
X3 Visual model fidelity 3/3
Visual model is mathematically accurate, colour-consistent across all screens, and matches the parent module's representations. Full marks.
X7 Sound & celebration 2/2
Audio spec is complete, purposeful, and correctly tied to mastery milestones. Full marks.
L — Language · 3
L4 Geographic viability 3/3
Full marks. All event card contexts are culturally authentic for Indonesian Grade 9 students. The 'Pak. President' reference uses the correct Indonesian honorific. No Western-centric or geographically inappropriate contexts are present. The convention_requirement for Indonesian event card contexts is satisfied.
L7 Age-appropriate design 2/2
Full marks. The design is appropriately mature for Grade 9 — no mascots, no infantilising language, no over-gamified reward mechanics. The math speaks through the reasoning task itself.
L8 Translation survivability 1/1
Full mark. All student-facing text is in editable text elements, no idioms are present, and the content is structured for straightforward localisation.
Gate Checks 5 passed, 2 failed
PASS
G1 — Clear LO Hard gate
Applet has a specific, testable LO scoped narrower than or equal to the module LO.
Slide 1 title 'HIGH Chance or LOW Chance?' and body copy 'Let's look at some uncertain events and sort them based on their chances – high chance of happening or low chance of happening' establish a clear, testable LO: students sort uncertain event cards into HIGH Chance or LOW Chance categories. This is narrower than the module LO (which covers the full chance spectrum) and directly matches the blueprint LO: 'Sort uncertain events into HIGH Chance or LOW Chance categories by dragging event cards, demonstrating that chance varies across situations.'
PASS
G3 — Every interaction serves thinking Hard gate
No interaction is completable without concept-linked thought — no robot-completable sequences.
Screen 1 (Slide 1): 'Tap Start to begin' is a launch gate, not a thinking interaction — acceptable as a session-entry affordance. Screen 6 (Slides 6–10): The core drag interaction requires students to judge whether each event card belongs in HIGH Chance or LOW Chance. Slide 7 shows 'You will eat rice today' with scaffolding text 'Most students in Indonesia eat rice every day – this has a high chance of happening' before the drag, so the first card is guided. Slide 9 shows 'Your teacher will cancel all classes for a week' with no scaffolding text visible (only a dev note), requiring independent reasoning. Slide 10 shows incorrect-placement feedback 'Think about how often this happens. Almost never – this has a very low chance' — this is a frequency-reasoning prompt, not a give-away. Screen 7 (Slides 11–12): 'Tap Conclude' after viewing all sorted cards requires the student to verify the full sort before seeing the summary. No interaction is mechanically completable without engaging with the event content. The first card (rice) is over-scaffolded (answer given in the scaffolding text before the drag), which reduces the thinking demand for that card, but the remaining cards require genuine reasoning.
PASS
G4 — Concept before procedure Hard gate
Learner understands WHY before learning HOW — concept is established before the procedural interaction begins.
Slide 1 establishes the conceptual frame ('Let's look at some uncertain events and sort them based on their chances – high chance of happening or low chance of happening') before any drag interaction. Slides 2–5 are developer-facing setup slides (dev notes, event table, audio spec, image references) — not student-facing. Slide 6 introduces the mechanic ('Tap Reveal Card') with the conceptual framing already in the title bar ('Identify if the given event has a HIGH chance or a LOW chance'). Slide 7 provides the first card with an explicit scaffolding rationale ('Most students in Indonesia eat rice every day – this has a high chance of happening') before asking the student to drag. The WHY (chance varies across uncertain events) is present in the framing before the HOW (drag to zone) is exercised.
PASS
G5 — State machine readable Hard gate
Developer can build the applet from the storyboard alone without calling the designer — all interaction states, feedback variants, and transitions are specified.
Screen 6 (Slides 6–10, animation_group_id 1): Slide 6 dev note 'Show the card with zoom animation' specifies the reveal animation. Slide 8 dev note is not visible in the thumbnail but the screen_map confirms feedback_slides=[10]. Slide 9 dev note 'Show next card with zoom animation' specifies the second card reveal. Slide 10 dev note 'If this question is placed in low chance column, then shake it – move it back and show incorrect feedback' specifies the incorrect-placement state machine for the teacher-cancels-classes card. Slide 4 (dev-notes table) provides the full event list with correct/incorrect feedback strings for all 10 questions, covering the complete state machine for the card loop. Slide 2 dev notes specify: show only 1 question at a time, add feedback space below, randomise event order. Slide 3 specifies audio feedback states (correct tone, incorrect tone, final fanfare, confetti). Screen 7 (Slides 11–12): Slide 11 shows the full sort with 'Conclude' button and a feedback string ('Right! A presidential visit is extremely rare – this has a very low chance') visible in the bottom bar, indicating the last-card confirmation state. Slide 12 is the completion screen. Taken together, a developer has sufficient information to implement the full interaction without designer consultation.
FAIL
G6 — Screen content self-sufficient Hard gate
Visible content (action instruction + scaffolding text + dev notes) carries the interaction without external guidance.
Screen 6, Slide 7: The scaffolding text 'Most students in Indonesia eat rice every day – this has a high chance of happening' appears BEFORE the student drags the card, effectively giving away the correct answer for the first card. This makes the first interaction non-self-sufficient in the opposite direction — the student does not need to think, they just confirm what the text already told them. The screen is self-sufficient for the developer but over-scaffolded for the student: the action instruction ('Drag that left or right based on its chance of happening') and the scaffolding text together remove the cognitive demand. Screen 6, Slide 9: The card 'Your teacher will cancel all classes for a week' appears with no student-facing scaffolding text visible (only a dev note), and no action instruction is visible in the thumbnail — the bottom bar is empty. A student encountering this card after the guided first card has no prompt to reason from. The transition from fully scaffolded (Slide 7) to unscaffolded (Slide 9) is abrupt and not self-sufficient for the student. Screen 7, Slide 11: The bottom feedback bar shows 'Right! A presidential visit is extremely rare – this has a very low chance' — this appears to be the feedback for the last card placed, but it is shown simultaneously with the full sort view and the 'Conclude' button, creating ambiguity about whether the student has already placed the presidential visit card or is being told to place it.
Recommendation: Fix Slide 7: Remove or reframe the pre-drag scaffolding text so it prompts reasoning rather than giving the answer. Replace 'Most students in Indonesia eat rice every day – this has a high chance of happening' with a frequency-reasoning prompt: 'Think: how often do most students in Indonesia eat rice for lunch?' — consistent with the blueprint's Repair-Guided Feedback archetype. Fix Slide 9: Add a student-facing action instruction to the bottom bar: 'Drag the card into the correct category.' and a reasoning prompt: 'Think about how often this happens for most students.' Fix Slide 11: Separate the last-card feedback state from the full-sort Conclude screen — show the feedback for the final card placement first, then advance to the full sort + Conclude view, so the two states are not conflated.
FAIL
G7 — Screen zones present Hard gate
Every interactive screen has all three layers: action instruction + scaffolding text + dev notes.
Screen 1 (Slide 1 — interactive, student_action): Action instruction present ('Tap Start to begin'). Scaffolding text present ('Let's look at some uncertain events and sort them based on their chances'). Dev notes: absent (screen_map confirms has_notes=false). However, Slide 1 is a launch/entry screen — the 'Tap Start' is a session-entry affordance, not a reasoning interaction. Marginal pass for this screen. Screen 6 (Slides 6–10 — interactive, student_action): Slide 6: Action instruction present ('Tap Reveal Card'). Scaffolding text present ('You will get a card with an event. Drag that left or right based on its chance of happening'). Dev note present ('Show the card with zoom animation'). PASS. Slide 7: Action instruction present ('Drag the card into the HIGH chance category'). Scaffolding text present (pre-answer text). Dev notes: absent from thumbnail — screen_map confirms has_notes=false for this slide group overall, but Slide 6 and Slide 10 carry dev notes. Slide 9: Action instruction: 'Drag the card into the correct category' — present. Scaffolding text: absent from student-facing area (only dev note visible). Dev note present. PARTIAL FAIL — scaffolding text zone missing for student on Slide 9. Slide 10: Action instruction present. Scaffolding text present ('Think about how often this happens. Almost never – this has a very low chance'). Dev note present. PASS. Screen 7 (Slides 11–12 — interactive, student_action): Slide 11: Action instruction present ('Tap Conclude'). Scaffolding text: the bottom bar shows a feedback string for the last card, not a reasoning scaffold for the Conclude action. Dev notes: absent. The three-zone requirement is not fully met — no scaffolding text explaining WHY the student should conclude (what to look for in the full sort before tapping Conclude). Slide 12 is a non-interactive summary screen — exempt.
Recommendation: Fix Slide 9: Add student-facing scaffolding text to the bottom reasoning bar (currently empty in the student-facing area): e.g., 'Think about how often this happens for most students. Does it happen very often, or almost never?' This restores the three-zone structure (action instruction + scaffolding text + dev note) for this card state. Fix Slide 11: Add a scaffolding text prompt above the 'Conclude' button explaining what the student should do before tapping: e.g., 'Look at all the sorted cards. Do you agree with where each one is placed?' This gives the Conclude interaction a reasoning purpose rather than being a pure navigation tap. Add dev notes to Slide 11 specifying the Conclude interaction state (what happens when tapped, what the completion trigger is).
PASS
G8 — Completable in time Hard gate
Whole applet can be completed meaningfully within approximately 5–7 minutes, with thinking time present between interactions.
The applet contains 10 event cards (confirmed by Slide 2 dev note: 'similar to the reference applet with 10 questions in total'). Each card requires: (1) tap Reveal Card, (2) read the event, (3) drag to zone, (4) receive feedback. Estimated time per card: 20–40 seconds including reading and reasoning. Total for 10 cards: 3–7 minutes. Add ~30 seconds for the Launch screen and ~30 seconds for the Consolidate screen. Total estimated range: 4–8 minutes. The upper bound slightly exceeds the 5–7 minute target for students who deliberate on borderline cards, but the applet includes a 'Start Over' option (Slide 12) and the dev note specifies randomisation, suggesting the design anticipates variable completion times. The thinking time between interactions is present: each card is revealed one at a time (Slide 2 dev note: 'Show only 1 question on the screen'), and the drag interaction requires a deliberate placement decision before the next card appears. Marginal pass — the 10-card set is at the upper boundary; if borderline cards generate multiple incorrect attempts, total time could reach 8–10 minutes for some students.
Antipatterns Detected 3 detected: Click-to-reveal, Scaffolding lock-in, Action without reasoning
AP4 — Click-to-reveal
Slide 6 (Screen 6, first frame)
Slide 6 contains an explicit 'Tap Reveal Card' action instruction before the first card is shown. The 'Reveal Card' button tap reveals the first event card — content that could simply be present on screen from the start, since the card itself is the stimulus for the drag interaction. The reveal adds no cognitive value: the student has not yet done anything to earn the reveal, and the card content ('You will eat rice today') is not a surprise that requires anticipation-building. This is a click-to-reveal that delays the actual learning interaction (the drag) without pedagogical purpose. Note: subsequent cards (slides 8–9) use 'Next Card' which is a legitimate navigation affordance after a completed action — only the initial 'Reveal Card' on slide 6 is flagged.
Fix: Remove the 'Reveal Card' button from slide 6. Display the first event card immediately when the activity screen loads (with the zoom animation already specified in the dev note). The student's first action should be the drag, not a tap-to-reveal. This aligns with the Binary Chance Sort archetype where the card is the stimulus and the drag is the response — no intermediate reveal step is needed.
✎edithigh confidence
AP8 — Scaffolding lock-in
Slides 7–8 (Screen 6, worked example frames)
Slide 7 provides a fully guided first card: the instruction tells the student exactly where to drag ('Drag the card into the HIGH chance category') and the feedback text is pre-displayed ('Most students in Indonesia eat rice every day – this has a high chance of happening'). This means the student receives the answer and reasoning before making any independent judgment. Slide 8 then shows the card already placed correctly. The scaffolding (answer + reasoning pre-revealed) persists through the first card without the student demonstrating any competence. From slide 9 onward the student works independently — so the lock-in is limited to the first card, but it does mean the first item is a passive observation rather than an active classification attempt.
Fix: Restructure the first card (slide 7) as a guided attempt rather than a revealed answer: show the card and the two categories, prompt with a reasoning question ('Think: do most students in Indonesia eat rice every day?'), and let the student drag first. Show the explanatory feedback ('Most students in Indonesia eat rice every day – this has a high chance') only AFTER the student places the card — correct or incorrect. This preserves the scaffolding intent while requiring an active first attempt, consistent with the IAS Explore stage.
✎editmedium confidence
AP9 — Action without reasoning
Slides 9 and onward (Screen 6, independent practice frames)
From slide 9 onward, the instruction is 'Drag the card into the correct category' with no scaffolding text to guide what the student should think about when evaluating each new card. The only reasoning prompt appears in the incorrect-placement feedback (slide 10: 'Think about how often this happens'). For the remaining 8 cards after the worked example, there is no pre-action reasoning scaffold — students are told what to do (drag) but not what to think about (e.g., 'How often does this happen in real life?'). This is particularly important for M280
Misconception medium
Students treat chance as a vague linguistic label ('probably', 'maybe', 'unlikely') and resist comparing uncertain events with any precision. In this applet's binary-sort context, the risk is that students drag cards arbitrarily — treating HIGH and LOW as interchangeable labels — rather than reasoning about which situations genuinely have more chance. The applet surfaces this when students place events like 'rain in Bogor in January' in LOW Chance or 'helicopter landing at school tomorrow' in HIGH Chance without any reasoning anchor.
Design the feedback flow so that when a card is placed incorrectly, the system does NOT simply say 'wrong' — instead it prompts: 'Think about how often this usually happens. Does it happen most of the time, or almost never?' This forces the student to reason from frequency rather than from vague feeling. The Repair-Guided Feedback archetype (IAS id 34) is the correct vehicle.
 (the target misconception): students who treat likelihood as a vague feeling rather than a frequency judgment need a consistent reasoning prompt to build the habit.
Fix: Add a persistent scaffolding prompt beneath the action instruction on all independent practice frames (slides 9 onward): 'Think: how often does this actually happen?' This one-sentence reasoning anchor directs students toward frequency-based thinking (addressing M280
Misconception medium
Students treat chance as a vague linguistic label ('probably', 'maybe', 'unlikely') and resist comparing uncertain events with any precision. In this applet's binary-sort context, the risk is that students drag cards arbitrarily — treating HIGH and LOW as interchangeable labels — rather than reasoning about which situations genuinely have more chance. The applet surfaces this when students place events like 'rain in Bogor in January' in LOW Chance or 'helicopter landing at school tomorrow' in HIGH Chance without any reasoning anchor.
Design the feedback flow so that when a card is placed incorrectly, the system does NOT simply say 'wrong' — instead it prompts: 'Think about how often this usually happens. Does it happen most of the time, or almost never?' This forces the student to reason from frequency rather than from vague feeling. The Repair-Guided Feedback archetype (IAS id 34) is the correct vehicle.
) without giving away the answer. The prompt should appear before the student drags, not only in error feedback.
✎edithigh confidence
Slide Review
General observations
L7 — Age-appropriate design 2/2
Evidence
The applet is designed for Grade 9 (age ~14–15). The event cards use everyday school and social contexts ('Your teacher will cancel all classes', 'Someone in your class will yawn during a lesson', 'You will become a famous singer') that are relatable and age-appropriate without being childish. No mascots or cartoon characters are specified. The interaction mechanic (drag-and-sort) is appropriately mature — it is a reasoning task, not a gamified reward loop. The completion screen uses a clean summary format. The audio feedback (tones, chime, confetti) is mild and not infantilising. The 'Pak. President' reference treats students as aware of civic life, which is appropriate for Grade 9.
L8 — Translation survivability 1/1
Evidence
No hardcoded text is embedded in images — all student-facing text appears as editable text elements in the slide shapes (PLACEHOLDER, AUTO_SHAPE). The event card texts are authored as text strings, not baked into graphics. The zone labels ('HIGH Chance', 'LOW Chance') are text elements. The completion summary is a text block. No idioms or culture-locked expressions appear in the student-facing text. The 'Pak. President' reference is culturally specific but not an idiom — it is a proper honorific that would be retained or adapted in localisation. Dev notes are clearly separated from student-facing content and would not be translated.
Module-wide recommendations
These actions apply across many slides or to the module as a whole. They are not anchored to a single slide row in the grid below.
⊘ Must fix
Sources: P2, P6, D4, X1
INSERT after the Notice screen (new slide): Add an Articulation screen with a sentence-completion tap-to-select panel: 'Both events are [uncertain / certain] — but they do NOT have the [same / different] chance, because one happens [much more often / never] than the other.' Each bracketed group is a tap-to-select choice. After correct completion, add a 'Say it aloud!' prompt to activate the Shout_Out_Loud trope moment. This delivers the blueprint's Stage 4 Articulation screen, resolves P2, P6, D4, and X1 gaps, and ensures students generate the principle rather than receiving it passively.
Slide 1
↑ Should fix
Sources: D1, G2
INSERT after slide 1: Add a Launch observe-screen showing the Bogor rain vs helicopter contrast (two images with bold question marks) and the framing caption: 'Both of these could happen tomorrow — but do they have the SAME chance? We are sorting into HIGH and LOW to practise comparing chance — but remember, chance is a spectrum from impossible to certain.' This delivers the Stage 1 Launch observe-screen the blueprint specifies and the convention requirement for HIGH/LOW framing.
↑ Should fix
Sources: L1, D7, G2
EDIT slide 1 (Launch): Change 'Let's look at some uncertain events' to 'Let's look at some uncertain outcomes — situations where we cannot be sure what will happen.' Add a sub-caption below the sort instruction: 'Note: HIGH and LOW are a starting point — chance is actually a full spectrum from impossible to certain!' This delivers the vocabulary_status requirement for 'uncertain outcome' and the convention requirement for HIGH/LOW framing.
Scorer details (6 annotations, 0 cross-refs)
P1 — LO alignment & scope 3/3
Covers slides 1, 12
Evidence
The applet's LO — sort uncertain events into HIGH Chance or LOW Chance by dragging event cards — is atomic and tightly scoped. Slide 1 frames the task as 'sort them based on their chances — high chance of happening or low chance of happening.' Slide 12 closes with 'Chance — how often something could actually happen. Very often — HIGH chance. Rarely — LOW chance.' No extraneous features (no numerical probability, no chance line, no formal sample space) appear anywhere in the 12 slides. The applet stays within the concrete CPA phase and the binary discrimination LO throughout.
P2 — Six-stage progression 3/5
Covers slides 1–12
Evidence
Screen 1 (slide 1) provides a Launch/Explore framing — students read the task and tap Start.
D1 — Screen-to-screen continuity 3/4
Covers slides 1–12
Evidence
The overall flow is logical: Launch (slide 1) → dev-note screens (slides 2–5, non-student-facing) → scaffolded first card with guided feedback (slides 6–10, screen 6) → full sort + conclude (slide 11, screen 7) → completion summary (slide 12). Within screen 6, the animation sequence is coherent: reveal card → first card shown with feedback → next card prompt → second card shown → incorrect-placement feedback. The transition from screen 6 to screen 7 (Conclude) is smooth — all sorted cards are visible in their zones. However, two continuity gaps exist: (1) There is no dedicated Launch/observe screen that frames the productive conflict before the first drag interaction — the applet jumps from the title card (slide 1) directly into the drag mechanic (slide 6) with no Stage 1 motivational context screen as the blueprint specifies.
D5 — Meaningful segues 2/3
Covers slides 1, 6, 8, 12
Evidence
The transition from the title/launch screen (slide 1) to the drag interaction (slide 6) is bridged by the instruction text on slide 1 ('Let's look at some uncertain events and sort them based on their chances') — this is a functional but thin segue.
D7 — Module integration 2/4
Covers slides 1, 8–9, 12
Evidence
Entry prerequisites: the applet's launch screen (slide 1) does not activate the parent module's established concepts — there is no reference to the Bogor rain vs helicopter contrast from the parent module's Slides 8–9, no activation of the 'uncertain outcome' concept, and no bridge from the parent module's chance strip (Slide 12). The convention requirement for a HIGH/LOW framing note at Launch is also unmet (the completion screen has a partial version but the Launch screen does not). The applet uses 'chance' exclusively throughout all student-facing text — the false-friend convention is correctly observed.
L2 — Action instruction clarity 2/3
Covers slides 1–12
Evidence
Slide 1 (Launch/Screen 1) provides a clear action instruction: 'Tap 'Start' to begin.' Slides 6–10 (Screen 6, the main sort interaction) provide: 'Tap 'Reveal Card'' and 'Drag the card into the correct category' and 'You will get a card with an event.
StageStage 1 — Launch Observe observe full Concrete
Concept
drag uncertain event cards into a HIGH Chance or LOW Chance zone using frequency reasoning as the basis for their judgment
state that 'chance' describes how often something could actually happen, with HIGH chance corresponding to events that happen very often and LOW chance to events that happen rarely
recognise that not all uncertain events are equally likely, using the rice-eating and presidential-visit contrast as reference examples
apply frequency-anchored reasoning when corrected on an incorrect placement (prompted by 'Think about how often this happens'). The applet delivers a functional binary sort with culturally authentic I
Vocab in screenused chance used uncertain outcome
ConventionHIGH / LOW as binary labels — risk of false precision
HIGH / LOW as binary labels — risk of false precision
The applet must include an explicit framing note at Launch (Stage 1) and a closing note at Consolidate (Stage 6) acknowledging that HIGH and LOW are a scaffold, not the complete model. Required text (
Slide 2
↑ Should fix
Sources: X4, X5, L5, L6, G5
EDIT slide 2 (dev notes) or INSERT a dedicated dev-note slide: Add comprehensive specifications covering: (a) Drag affordance: 'When a card is lifted, it enlarges to 1.1× its resting size and gains a drop shadow. Both drop zones pulse with a coloured border during drag — green pulse for HIGH Chance zone, amber/orange pulse for LOW Chance zone. Pulse deactivates on card release.' (b) Colour convention: 'HIGH Chance zone background = green (#4CAF50 or equivalent). LOW Chance zone background = amber/orange (#FF9800 or equivalent). Do NOT use red for LOW Chance — red carries a punitive connotation inconsistent with IAS visual-state conventions. Incorrect-placement border flash = orange, not red.' (c) Snap animation: 'On correct placement, card snaps to zone with a green tick (HIGH) or amber tick (LOW) and a brief affirming message.' (d) Touch target sizes: 'All tap targets (buttons) must meet a minimum touch target size of 44×44px per WCAG 2.1 SC 2.5.5. Draggable event cards must have a minimum drag-handle area of 44×44px. Drop zones must be large enough to accept a card drop without precision — minimum zone width 40% of screen width each.' (e) Accessibility: 'All draggable event cards must carry an aria-label matching the card text. Drop zones must carry aria-label="HIGH Chance drop zone" and "LOW Chance drop zone". Buttons must carry aria-label matching button text. The shake animation must respect the prefers-reduced-motion media query — substitute a static orange border highlight for users with reduced-motion preference. Zone background colours must achieve WCAG AA contrast ratio (≥4.5:1) against card text.'
Scorer details (4 annotations, 1 cross-refs)
P2 — Six-stage progression 3/5
Covers slides 1–12
Evidence
Screens 2–5 (slides 2–5) are developer-facing notes and image references, not student-facing stages; they consume structural space without delivering pedagogical stages.
P5 — Variation discipline 2/3
Covers slides 2, 11
Evidence
However, the developer notes (slide 2) specify 10 questions total but only 8 are visible on slide 11, and the instruction 'RANDOMISE ALL EVENTS — DO NOT GO IN THE ORDER OF ONE HIGH ONE LOW' is present but the storyboard does not show the full randomised set or confirm that borderline cases (cards requiring reasoning rather than extreme-case recognition) are included. The first guided card ('You will eat rice today') is a clear HIGH; the second card shown ('Your teacher will cancel all classes for a week') is a clear LOW — the contrast is extreme rather than graduated. No borderline cards (e.g., 'You will score 100% on your next maths test') appear in the visible storyboard frames.
L2 — Action instruction clarity 2/3
Covers slides 1–12
Evidence
The critical gap is on slides 2–5 (dev-note-only screens): these are not student-facing, so no action instruction is needed there.
L5 — Touch target & ergonomics 2/3
Covers slides 2, 6–11
Evidence
The dev notes on slide 2 specify a zoom animation on card reveal (slide 9: 'Show next card with zoom animation') which aids visual affordance. However, no dev note specifies minimum touch target sizes for the draggable cards or the tap buttons. The blueprint's IAS drag affordance (card enlarges to 1.1× during drag) is not specified in the dev notes, meaning the developer has no guidance on the ergonomic drag state.
D1 — Screen-to-screen continuity see Slide 1

recognise that not all uncertain events are equally likely, using the rice-eating and presidential-visit contrast as reference examples

Visual modelVIR 3 Two-zone drop area (Binary Chance Sort layout): LEFT zone labelled 'HIGH Chance' with green background and upward arrow icon; RIGHT zone labelled 'LOW Chance' with amber/orange background and downward arrow icon. A vertical card stack sits in the centre. First two cards are presented one at a time: Card 1 — 'It will rain in Bogor tomorrow in January'; Card 2 — 'A helicopter will land at your school tomorrow'. When a card is lifted, both zones pulse with coloured borders (green pulse for HIGH, orange pulse for LOW). Card enlarges to 1.1× with drop shadow during drag (IAS drag affordance). A small helper banner above the stack reads: 'Think: does this happen most of the time, or almost never?' Concrete

chance vs probability — applet feedback strings

 Indonesian event card contexts
Indonesian event card contexts
All six event cards must use authentic Indonesian everyday contexts. Required card set: (1) Rain in Bogor in January — HIGH; (2) Helicopter landing at school tomorrow — LOW; (3) Eating rice for lunch
Colour coding consistency with parent module
Colour coding consistency with parent module
The HIGH Chance drop zone must use green; the LOW Chance drop zone must use amber/orange. Do not use red for the LOW Chance zone — red carries a punitive connotation inconsistent with IAS visual-state
Slide 3
Gaps noted here — fixes at: X2 → Slide 10 · X4 → Slide 6 · L6 → Slide 2
Scorer observations (4 criteria noted this slide)
X2 — Feedback design 3/4
Covers slides 3–4, 7, 10–11
Evidence
Feedback is present and event-specific for at least two cards. A presidential visit is extremely rare — this has a very low chance.' Audio feedback is fully specified in the dev notes (slide 3): rising tone for correct, descending for incorrect, chime on final card, confetti on completion — all purposeful and diagnostic.
X4 — Animation as operation 2/3
Covers slides 3, 6, 9–10
Evidence
Audio feedback timing is specified (0.5s rising tone per correct placement — slide 3). However, the drag operation itself — the core conceptual action of physically moving a card to a zone — has no explicit animation specification for the drag state (card enlarges to 1.1×, gains shadow, zones pulse). The blueprint specifies zone-pulse affordance during drag (IAS Highlight/Pulse/Emphasis) but this is absent from the storyboard dev notes. Transition speed for card snap-to-zone is not specified. The animation-as-operation principle (movement performs the concept) is partially met: the zoom reveal and shake-on-error are purposeful, but the drag-state animation that makes the sorting operation feel conceptually meaningful is unspecified.
X7 — Sound & celebration 2/2
Evidence
Audio feedback is fully specified in slide 3 dev notes: (1) short rising tone (0.5s) on each correct card placement, (2) short descending tone on incorrect placement accompanying shake animation, (3) distinct chime or fanfare on final card correctly sorted, (4) brief confetti burst or star animation on the completion screen tied to full activity completion — not triggered on Start Over. Rising tones for correct, descending for incorrect — purposeful and diagnostic. Celebration (confetti/star) is tied to mastery milestone (full activity completion), not individual clicks. The 'not triggered on Start Over' specification correctly prevents celebration inflation.
L6 — Accessibility 2/3
Covers slides 3, 10
Evidence
The storyboard specifies audio feedback (slide 3 dev notes) with four distinct audio states — this provides a non-visual feedback channel for correct/incorrect placement, which is a positive accessibility signal. The colour coding convention (green = HIGH, amber/orange = LOW) is referenced in the blueprint and research_brief but not explicitly specified in the dev notes with contrast ratios. No alt-text or screen-reader labels are specified for the draggable event cards, drop zones, or button elements.
P2 — Six-stage progression see Slide 1
D1 — Screen-to-screen continuity see Slide 1
L2 — Action instruction clarity see Slide 1
StageStage 3 — Notice Discriminate observe partial Concrete
Concept
drag uncertain event cards into a HIGH Chance or LOW Chance zone using frequency reasoning as the basis for their judgment
state that 'chance' describes how often something could actually happen, with HIGH chance corresponding to events that happen very often and LOW chance to events that happen rarely
apply frequency-anchored reasoning when corrected on an incorrect placement (prompted by 'Think about how often this happens'). The applet delivers a functional binary sort with culturally authentic I
Vocab in screenused chance used uncertain outcome

Visual modelVIR 3 The two anchor cards (rain in Bogor / helicopter at school) are now shown side by side in their correct zones, with a spotlight/highlight effect. A comparison panel appears between them with the text: 'Both are uncertain — neither is impossible or certain. But are they equally likely?' Two frequency bars (non-numerical, purely visual — a tall green bar for rain, a very short orange bar for helicopter) appear beneath each card to represent 'how often this happens'. A prompt question appears: 'What do you notice about these two events?' Concrete

Visual modelVIR 3 The two anchor cards remain visible in their zones (smaller, at top of screen). Below them, a sentence-completion panel appears: 'Both events are [uncertain / certain / impossible] — but they do NOT have the [same / different] chance, because one happens [much more often / never / sometimes] than the other.' Each bracketed group is a tap-to-select choice (students tap the correct word from 2–3 options). After completing the sentence, a 'Say it aloud!' prompt appears (Shout_Out_Loud echo): 'Read your completed sentence to a partner!' Concrete

↑ Should fix
Sources: P5, X2, D2
EDIT slide 5 (image references): Confirm that the 10-card set includes at least 2 borderline cases in the storyboard (e.g., 'You will score 100% on your next maths test' — LOW but not impossible; 'At least one student in your class will be absent this week' — HIGH but requires reasoning about class size and frequency). Ensure these borderline cards appear in the storyboard sequence so the variation arc is documented. Add event-specific 'This one is tricky!' feedback variants for borderline cards.

Visual modelVIR 3 Full two-zone sort layout returns: HIGH Chance (green, left) and LOW Chance (amber, right) drop zones. The anchor pair cards are already shown in their correct zones as reference. A new stack of 4 remaining event cards appears in the centre: Card 3 — 'You will eat rice for lunch today'; Card 4 — 'You will score 100% on your next maths test'; Card 5 — 'At least one student in your class will be absent this week'; Card 6 — 'You will find a 50,000 rupiah note on the ground today'. The helper banner is REMOVED (scaffolding withdrawn). Zone-pulse affordance remains active during drag. Cards include 2 borderline cases (Cards 3 and 5 are HIGH but require reasoning; Cards 4 and 6 are LOW but not impossible) to surface M283. Concrete
Screen 6 — Slides 6–10 Interactive screen (Student Action)
Feedback flow: slides 10 (happy/unhappy paths)
Slide 6
Slide 7
Slide 8
Slide 9
Slide 10
⊘ Must fix
Sources: AP4, X4, X5, G5
EDIT slide 6: Remove the 'Reveal Card' button and display the first event card immediately when the activity screen loads (with the zoom animation already specified in the dev note). Add an orange accent border or drag-handle icon to the event card to signal it is draggable. Add a dev note specifying the drag-state animation: 'When a card is lifted, enlarge it to 1.1× with a drop shadow. Both drop zones pulse with a coloured border during drag (green pulse for HIGH Chance zone, orange pulse for LOW Chance zone). On correct release, card snaps to zone with a 300ms ease-in animation. On incorrect release, card returns to centre stack with a 200ms shake animation.' This resolves AP4 (click-to-reveal), X4 (animation as operation), X5 (affordance clarity), and G5 state-machine completeness.
⊘ Must fix
Sources: AP8, G6, D4
EDIT slide 7: Remove the pre-revealed answer. Replace the scaffolding text 'Most students in Indonesia eat rice every day — this has a high chance of happening' with a frequency-reasoning prompt: 'Think: how often do most students in Indonesia eat rice for lunch?' Let the student drag first, then show the explanatory feedback ('Most students in Indonesia eat rice every day — this has a high chance') only AFTER the student places the card (correct or incorrect). This resolves AP8 (scaffolding lock-in) and G6 (screen content self-sufficient).
⊘ Must fix
Sources: P2, P3, D4, D5, X1
INSERT after slide 7 (after the first card is correctly placed): Add a Notice screen showing the rice card and the teacher-cancels card side by side in their correct zones with qualitative frequency bars beneath each (tall green bar for HIGH, short orange bar for LOW — non-numerical, purely visual). Include a brief prompt: 'Both are uncertain — but are they equally likely? What do you notice?' (no student input required, tap to continue). This delivers the blueprint's Stage 3 Notice screen and directly targets M281
Misconception high
Students believe all uncertain events are equally likely — if something can happen or not happen, they default to 50/50. In a binary HIGH/LOW sort, this manifests as students placing all uncertain events in the same category (typically HIGH, since 'it could happen') or expressing that the sort is arbitrary because 'both could happen'. This is the highest-severity misconception for this applet and the primary reason the binary sort interaction was designed.
Include at least two event-card pairs in the sort set where one event is clearly HIGH and the other clearly LOW, drawn from the same domain (e.g., 'It will rain in Bogor tomorrow in January' vs 'A helicopter will land at your school tomorrow'). When a student places both in the same category, the feedback should contrast the two: 'Both are uncertain — but are they equally likely? Think about how often each one actually happens.' The Contrastive Feedback archetype (IAS id 32) is the correct vehicle for this pair-level feedback.
.
↑ Should fix
Sources: X6, L3
EDIT slide 8 (transition to independent practice): Replace the scaffolding text 'Now it's your turn — drag each card to the right category' with a reasoning prompt: 'Think: does this happen most of the time, or almost never?' This shifts the scaffolding from action-guidance to reasoning-guidance and ensures consistent frequency-anchored thinking across all independent frames.
⊘ Must fix
Sources: G7, AP9, X6
EDIT slide 9: Add a student-facing action instruction to the bottom bar: 'Drag the card into the correct category.' Add a reasoning prompt: 'Think about how often this happens for most students. Does it happen very often, or almost never?' This restores the three-zone structure (action instruction + scaffolding text + dev note) and resolves G7 (screen zones present) and AP9 (action without reasoning).
↑ Should fix
Sources: X2, L3, P5
EDIT slide 10: Add a second feedback variant for borderline cards (e.g., 'At least one student absent this month' or 'Someone will yawn during a lesson'): 'This one is tricky! Think about how often this happens for most students your age.' This acknowledges partial correctness difficulty and matches the research_brief's M283
Misconception medium
Students can identify impossible and certain events but treat all uncertain events as vague and undifferentiated — they cannot reason about relative likelihood between uncertain events. In the binary sort, this surfaces as random placement of uncertain events, or as students correctly placing the extreme cases (if any appear) but failing on the middle-range events. The applet's binary sort is a simplified version of the full ordering task — it reduces the cognitive demand to a two-category decision, which is the appropriate scaffold at this concrete phase.
Include 2–3 event cards that are 'borderline' (e.g., 'You will eat rice for lunch today' — HIGH for most Indonesian students; 'You will score 100% on your next test' — LOW but not impossible). These borderline cards force students to reason rather than rely on extreme-case recognition. Feedback for borderline cards should acknowledge the reasoning challenge: 'This one is tricky! Think about how often this happens for most students your age.' The Repair-Guided Feedback archetype (IAS id 34) handles this.
 surfacing strategy. Also author event-specific frequency-reasoning feedback strings for all 10 sort cards and add them to the storyboard (slides 6–11 interaction states). Each string should follow the pattern: 'Think about how often this happens. [Frequency observation] – this has a [HIGH/LOW] chance.'
StageStage 6 — Consolidate Verify observe independent Concrete
Concept
drag uncertain event cards into a HIGH Chance or LOW Chance zone using frequency reasoning as the basis for their judgment
recognise that not all uncertain events are equally likely, using the rice-eating and presidential-visit contrast as reference examples
apply frequency-anchored reasoning when corrected on an incorrect placement (prompted by 'Think about how often this happens'). The applet delivers a functional binary sort with culturally authentic I
state that 'chance' describes how often something could actually happen, with HIGH chance corresponding to events that happen very often and LOW chance to events that happen rarely

M280 eff 3/3
M280 — treating chance as a vague linguistic label, resisting frequency-based comparison
how: Slide 7 frequency-anchored feedback: 'Most students in Indonesia eat rice every day — this has a high chance of happening'; Slide 10 repair-guided feedback: 'Think about how often this happens. Almost
Visual modelVIR 3 Contrastive Pair Reveal panel: the two anchor cards (rain in Bogor / helicopter at school) are shown side by side in their correct zones with a spotlight. Below them, a summary explanation: 'Both are uncertain — but rain in Bogor in January happens very often, while a helicopter landing at school almost never does. That is why their chance is different.' Below the contrastive reveal, a compact summary strip shows all 6 sorted cards in their correct zones (a miniature version of the full sort). A closing framing note appears: 'Chance is a spectrum — from impossible to certain. HIGH and LOW are a starting point. In the next lesson, you will place events more precisely on a chance line.' A 'Done ✓' button completes the applet. Concrete
Screen 7 — Slides 11–12 Interactive screen (Student Action)

Slide 11
Slide 12
↑ Should fix
Sources: D3, D7, X8, P3
SPLIT slide 11 into two screens: (a) a 'Sort complete' confirmation screen showing all 8 cards in their zones with no feedback text — student reviews their work; (b) a Consolidate screen showing the contrastive pair reveal (rice vs teacher-cancels or rice vs presidential visit spotlighted) with qualitative frequency bars, the closing explanation 'Both are uncertain — but their chance is very different. That is why we cannot treat all uncertain events the same.', and the 'Conclude' CTA. This reduces cognitive load at consolidation (D3), delivers the contrastive pair reveal the blueprint requires (D7, X8), and provides the conceptual bridge to the parent module's Exit_Question.
↑ Should fix
Sources: L1, L2, D7, G2
EDIT slide 12 (completion screen): Add one line: 'These are all uncertain outcomes — we cannot predict them with certainty.' Add a forward-bridge sentence: 'In the next lesson, you will place events more precisely on a chance line — HIGH and LOW are just the beginning!' Add a 'Done ✓' button with the instruction text: 'Tap Done to continue.' This delivers the vocabulary_status requirement for 'uncertain outcome' (L1), resolves the navigation gap (L2), and bridges to M2's chance line without scope creep.
StageStage 6 — Consolidate Verify observe independent Concrete

state that 'chance' describes how often something could actually happen, with HIGH chance corresponding to events that happen very often and LOW chance to events that happen rarely
recognise that not all uncertain events are equally likely, using the rice-eating and presidential-visit contrast as reference examples
apply frequency-anchored reasoning when corrected on an incorrect placement (prompted by 'Think about how often this happens'). The applet delivers a functional binary sort with culturally authentic I

Misc addressedM281 eff 0/3
M281 eff 0/3
Visual modelVIR 3 Contrastive Pair Reveal panel: the two anchor cards (rain in Bogor / helicopter at school) are shown side by side in their correct zones with a spotlight. Below them, a summary explanation: 'Both are uncertain — but rain in Bogor in January happens very often, while a helicopter landing at school almost never does. That is why their chance is different.' Below the contrastive reveal, a compact summary strip shows all 6 sorted cards in their correct zones (a miniature version of the full sort). A closing framing note appears: 'Chance is a spectrum — from impossible to certain. HIGH and LOW are a starting point. In the next lesson, you will place events more precisely on a chance line.' A 'Done ✓' button completes the applet. Concrete VIR completion summary with chance definition abstract
After completing this applet with the recommended upgrades, students will be able to: (1) drag uncertain event cards into HIGH Chance or LOW Chance zones using frequency reasoning as the basis for their judgment; (2) articulate in their own words that uncertain events differ in chance because some happen more often than others; (3) notice and name the contrast between two sorted events through a structured side-by-side comparison with qualitative frequency bars; (4) apply frequency-anchored reasoning independently to new uncertain events without scaffolding. The applet will deliver the full six-stage arc (Launch → Explore → Notice → Explain → Apply → Consolidate) required for a Learn-column consolidation applet.
Mastery Assessment 55% coverage
Coverage 55%
The applet delivers its core drag-sort mechanic with culturally authentic Indonesian event cards and frequency-anchored feedback for two demonstrated cards. However, three of the six blueprint stages (Notice, Articulate, and a clean graduated Apply) are absent from the delivered storyboard. The Articulation stage — where students generate the principle — is entirely missing. The contrastive pair reveal at consolidation (the primary M281
Misconception high
Students believe all uncertain events are equally likely — if something can happen or not happen, they default to 50/50. In a binary HIGH/LOW sort, this manifests as students placing all uncertain events in the same category (typically HIGH, since 'it could happen') or expressing that the sort is arbitrary because 'both could happen'. This is the highest-severity misconception for this applet and the primary reason the binary sort interaction was designed.
Include at least two event-card pairs in the sort set where one event is clearly HIGH and the other clearly LOW, drawn from the same domain (e.g., 'It will rain in Bogor tomorrow in January' vs 'A helicopter will land at your school tomorrow'). When a student places both in the same category, the feedback should contrast the two: 'Both are uncertain — but are they equally likely? Think about how often each one actually happens.' The Contrastive Feedback archetype (IAS id 32) is the correct vehicle for this pair-level feedback.
 confrontation moment) is absent. Borderline cards targeting M283
Misconception medium
Students can identify impossible and certain events but treat all uncertain events as vague and undifferentiated — they cannot reason about relative likelihood between uncertain events. In the binary sort, this surfaces as random placement of uncertain events, or as students correctly placing the extreme cases (if any appear) but failing on the middle-range events. The applet's binary sort is a simplified version of the full ordering task — it reduces the cognitive demand to a two-category decision, which is the appropriate scaffold at this concrete phase.
Include 2–3 event cards that are 'borderline' (e.g., 'You will eat rice for lunch today' — HIGH for most Indonesian students; 'You will score 100% on your next test' — LOW but not impossible). These borderline cards force students to reason rather than rely on extreme-case recognition. Feedback for borderline cards should acknowledge the reasoning challenge: 'This one is tricky! Think about how often this happens for most students your age.' The Repair-Guided Feedback archetype (IAS id 34) handles this.
 are specified in dev notes but not confirmed in storyboard frames. Two hard gates failed (G6: screen content not self-sufficient; G7: screen zones incomplete on slides 9 and 11). Three antipatterns are present (AP4: click-to-reveal on slide 6; AP8: scaffolding lock-in on slide 7; AP9: action without reasoning from slide 9 onward). The applet's own blueprint scoped six stages and three misconceptions; the delivered content covers roughly half the planned interaction arc and two of three misconceptions with partial depth.
What students CAN do after this module
After completing this applet as reviewed, students can: (1) drag uncertain event cards into a HIGH Chance or LOW Chance zone using frequency reasoning as the basis for their judgment; (2) state that 'chance' describes how often something could actually happen, with HIGH chance corresponding to events that happen very often and LOW chance to events that happen rarely; (3) recognise that not all uncertain events are equally likely, using the rice-eating and presidential-visit contrast as reference examples; (4) apply frequency-anchored reasoning when corrected on an incorrect placement (prompted by 'Think about how often this happens'). The applet delivers a functional binary sort with culturally authentic Indonesian event cards, consistent colour coding, and purposeful audio feedback.
Fluency gaps — what students CANNOT reliably do
Students cannot yet: (1) articulate the principle that uncertain events differ in chance in their own words — the Articulation stage (sentence-completion panel) is absent, so the closing summary does the articulation for the student rather than eliciting it; (2) notice and name the contrast between two sorted events through a structured discriminative pause — the Notice stage (contrastive pair side-by-side with qualitative frequency bars) is absent; (3) apply frequency reasoning independently without a reasoning scaffold — from slide 9 onward, no pre-action reasoning prompt is present (AP9: action without reasoning), meaning students are told what to do but not what to think; (4) use 'uncertain outcome' as a compound term — the applet uses 'uncertain events' and 'uncertain situation' but never delivers 'uncertain outcome' as a unit; (5) confront M281
Misconception high
Students believe all uncertain events are equally likely — if something can happen or not happen, they default to 50/50. In a binary HIGH/LOW sort, this manifests as students placing all uncertain events in the same category (typically HIGH, since 'it could happen') or expressing that the sort is arbitrary because 'both could happen'. This is the highest-severity misconception for this applet and the primary reason the binary sort interaction was designed.
Include at least two event-card pairs in the sort set where one event is clearly HIGH and the other clearly LOW, drawn from the same domain (e.g., 'It will rain in Bogor tomorrow in January' vs 'A helicopter will land at your school tomorrow'). When a student places both in the same category, the feedback should contrast the two: 'Both are uncertain — but are they equally likely? Think about how often each one actually happens.' The Contrastive Feedback archetype (IAS id 32) is the correct vehicle for this pair-level feedback.
 (equally-likely default) through a structured interaction — the contrastive pair reveal at consolidation is absent, so the key misconception is only partially surfaced through the completed sort display rather than a dedicated confrontation moment; (6) navigate forward from the applet to the parent module's Exit_Question — no 'Done ✓' button exists, leaving students without a clear exit path.
Conceptual depth
2/5 — Students encounter the WHY of chance variation through frequency-anchored feedback strings (slides 7 and 10: 'Most students in Indonesia eat rice every day — this has a high chance of happening' / 'Think about how often this happens. Almost never — this has a very low chance'), which is a genuine conceptual anchor. However, the applet compresses the six-stage arc to Launch → Manipulate → Consolidate, skipping the Notice and Articulation stages where the WHY is made explicit by the student rather than received passively. The closing summary (slide 12) states the principle ('Every uncertain situation does NOT have the same chance') but the student never generates it. The absence of qualitative frequency bars (the pictorial representation that would make the frequency reasoning visible) means the WHY remains embedded in text strings rather than made structurally visible. Two hard gates failed (G6, G7) confirm that the screen content does not fully carry the interaction without external guidance.
Transfer readiness
2/5 — Students sort 8–10 randomised Indonesian event cards, which provides some breadth of application. However, the scaffolding arc is compressed: the transition from the fully guided first card (rice, answer pre-revealed) to the independent full sort is abrupt, with no intermediate partially-scaffolded cards confirmed in the storyboard. Borderline cards (events requiring reasoning rather than extreme-case recognition) are specified in dev notes but not confirmed in storyboard frames, so the variation arc may not include the graduated middle range needed for transfer. The absence of the Articulation stage means students have not generated the principle themselves, reducing the likelihood of transfer to novel contexts. The applet does not bridge to the parent module's Exit_Question (Pak Budi's two bags), which is the primary transfer task.
Mastery path to 100
Students have no structured confrontation with M281 (equally-likely default) — the contrastive pair reveal at consolidation is absent, so the key misconception is only implicitly addressed through the completed sort display.
Closes the M281 gap (high severity), adds the pictorial CPA representation layer (resolving P3 gap), delivers the blueprint's Stage 3 and Stage 6 visual models, and provides the conceptual bridge to the parent module's Exit_Question.
Insert a dedicated Notice screen (blueprint Stage 3) after the second card is correctly placed: show the rice card and the teacher-cancels card side by side in their correct zones with qualitative frequency bars (tall green bar for HIGH, short amber bar for LOW — non-numerical) and the prompt 'Both are uncertain — but are they equally likely? What do you notice?' Then insert a Consolidate contrastive pair reveal on slide 12 (or a new screen before it) showing the two most contrasting cards spotlighted with the explanation 'Both are uncertain — but their chance is very different. That is why we cannot treat all uncertain events the same.'
Students never generate the principle in their own words — the Articulation stage (sentence-completion panel) is entirely absent, so the applet does the articulation for the student.
Closes the Articulation gap (P2, P6, D4 deductions), activates the Shout_Out_Loud trope, adds a third action type (tap-to-select) resolving the X1 interaction richness gap, and ensures students own the principle before the independent sort.
Insert an Articulation screen (blueprint Stage 4) after the Notice screen: a sentence-completion tap-to-select panel — 'Both events are [uncertain / certain] — but they do NOT have the [same / different] chance, because one happens [much more often / never] than the other.' After correct completion, add a 'Say it aloud!' prompt to activate the Shout_Out_Loud trope moment within the applet.
Students sort cards without a pre-action reasoning scaffold from slide 9 onward — they are told what to do (drag) but not what to think about (frequency reasoning), which is the primary habit M280 requires.
Resolves AP8 (scaffolding lock-in), AP9 (action without reasoning), and G6 (screen content self-sufficient) gate failure. Ensures every card interaction is concept-linked and frequency-anchored, directly addressing M280.
Add a persistent reasoning prompt beneath the action instruction on all independent practice frames (slides 9 onward): 'Think: how often does this actually happen?' Remove the pre-revealed answer from slide 7 (AP8 fix) and replace with a frequency-reasoning question prompt so the first card is an active attempt, not a passive observation. Fix slide 7 per G6 gate requirement.
Students have no independent transfer task with graduated difficulty — the transition from the guided first card to the full 8-card sort is abrupt, and borderline cards (requiring reasoning rather than extreme-case recognition) are not confirmed in the storyboard.
Closes M283 gap (medium severity), resolves D2 (difficulty gradation) and P5 (variation discipline) deductions, and creates the partial → guided → independent scaffolding arc the blueprint specifies.
After the guided first card (rice), add 1–2 intermediate cards with the frequency-reasoning helper banner still visible but no pre-revealed answer. Then remove the banner for the remaining cards. Confirm that the 10-card set includes at least 2 borderline cases in the storyboard (e.g., 'You will score 100% on your next maths test' — LOW; 'At least one student in your class will be absent this week' — HIGH) with 'This one is tricky!' feedback variants.
Students cannot navigate forward from the applet to the parent module's Exit_Question — only a 'Start Over' button exists on slide 12, with no 'Done ✓' forward-navigation instruction.
Resolves L2 deduction and ensures the applet's conceptual bridge to the Exit_Question (Pak Budi's two bags) is navigable. Without this, the teacher has no formative data from the Exit_Question for students who complete the applet.
Add a 'Done ✓' button to slide 12 with the instruction 'Tap Done to continue.' This returns control to the parent module's Exit_Question slide as specified in the blueprint's Stage 6 feedback_type.
Students never encounter 'uncertain outcome' as a compound term — the applet uses 'uncertain events' and 'uncertain situation' but not the required vocabulary unit.
Resolves L1 vocabulary gap and the knowledge_trace gap (uncertain outcome: not_introduced). Provides the compound term students need as a prerequisite for M3's formal vocabulary (experiment, trial, outcome, sample space).
On slide 1 (Launch), change 'Let's look at some uncertain events' to 'Let's look at some uncertain outcomes — situations where we cannot be sure what will happen.' On slide 12, add: 'These are all uncertain outcomes — we cannot predict them with certainty.'
Students have no framing note acknowledging that HIGH/LOW is a scaffold rather than the complete model — they may leave believing all events are either HIGH or LOW with no middle ground, contradicting the parent module's chance strip.
Resolves the blueprint convention requirement for HIGH/LOW framing, prevents the false-precision misconception, and bridges to M2's chance line (G9C4M2) without scope creep.
Add a brief framing note to slide 1 (Launch) below the sort instruction: 'Note: HIGH and LOW are a starting point — chance is actually a full spectrum from impossible to certain!' Add a forward-bridge sentence to slide 12: 'In the next lesson, you will place events more precisely on a chance line — HIGH and LOW are just the beginning!'
The drag-state animation spec is absent — developers have no guidance on card enlargement (1.1×), drop shadow, or zone-pulse colours during drag, meaning the IAS drag affordance cannot be built from the storyboard alone.
Resolves X4 (animation as operation), X5 (affordance clarity), L5 (touch target spec), and L6 (accessibility) deductions. Ensures the developer can build the full IAS drag affordance without designer consultation, resolving the partial G5 state-machine completeness.
Add a dev note to slide 6 specifying: 'When a card is lifted, enlarge to 1.1× with drop shadow. Both zones pulse with coloured borders during drag (green for HIGH, orange for LOW). On correct release, card snaps with 300ms ease-in. On incorrect release, 200ms shake and return to centre.' Also add ARIA labels for all interactive elements and a reduced-motion alternative for the shake animation.
Insert two new screens after the first guided card: (1) a Notice screen showing the rice and teacher-cancels cards side by side with qualitative frequency bars and the prompt 'Both are uncertain — but are they equally likely?'; (2) an Articulation screen with a sentence-completion tap-to-select panel ('Both events are [uncertain/certain] — but they do NOT have the [same/different] chance, because one happens [much more often/never] than the other'). These two insertions close the P2, P6, D4, and X1 gaps and directly address M281. Second priority: remove the pre-revealed answer from slide 7 and add persistent reasoning prompts ('Think: how often does this actually happen?') to all independent practice frames (slides 9 onward) to resolve AP8, AP9, and G6 failures.
Path to 100 — Priority Actions ⊘ Must fix 5 · ↑ Should fix 21 · 2 mastery gaps
⊘ Must fix G6 Screen content self-sufficient
Fix Slide 7: Remove or reframe the pre-drag scaffolding text so it prompts reasoning rather than giving the answer. Replace 'Most students in Indonesia eat rice every day – this has a high chance of happening' with a frequency-reasoning prompt: 'Think: how often do most students in Indonesia eat rice for lunch?' — consistent with the blueprint's Repair-Guided Feedback archetype. Fix Slide 9: Add a student-facing action instruction to the bottom bar: 'Drag the card into the correct category.' and a reasoning prompt: 'Think about how often this happens for most students.' Fix Slide 11: Separate the last-card feedback state from the full-sort Conclude screen — show the feedback for the final card placement first, then advance to the full sort + Conclude view, so the two states are not conflated.
Visible content (action instruction + scaffolding text + dev notes) carries the interaction without external guidance.
⊘ Must fix G7 Screen zones present
Fix Slide 9: Add student-facing scaffolding text to the bottom reasoning bar (currently empty in the student-facing area): e.g., 'Think about how often this happens for most students. Does it happen very often, or almost never?' This restores the three-zone structure (action instruction + scaffolding text + dev note) for this card state. Fix Slide 11: Add a scaffolding text prompt above the 'Conclude' button explaining what the student should do before tapping: e.g., 'Look at all the sorted cards. Do you agree with where each one is placed?' This gives the Conclude interaction a reasoning purpose rather than being a pure navigation tap. Add dev notes to Slide 11 specifying the Conclude interaction state (what happens when tapped, what the completion trigger is).
Every interactive screen has all three layers: action instruction + scaffolding text + dev notes.
⊘ Must fix✎edithigh confidence AP4 Click-to-reveal
Remove the 'Reveal Card' button from slide 6. Display the first event card immediately when the activity screen loads (with the zoom animation already specified in the dev note). The student's first action should be the drag, not a tap-to-reveal. This aligns with the Binary Chance Sort archetype where the card is the stimulus and the drag is the response — no intermediate reveal step is needed.
Slide 6 contains an explicit 'Tap Reveal Card' action instruction before the first card is shown. The 'Reveal Card' button tap reveals the first event card — content that could simply be present on screen from the start, since the card itself is the stimulus for the drag interaction. The reveal adds no cognitive value: the student has not yet done anything to earn the reveal, and the card content ('You will eat rice today') is not a surprise that requires anticipation-building. This is a click-to-reveal that delays the actual learning interaction (the drag) without pedagogical purpose. Note: subsequent cards (slides 8–9) use 'Next Card' which is a legitimate navigation affordance after a completed action — only the initial 'Reveal Card' on slide 6 is flagged.
⊘ Must fix✎editmedium confidence AP8 Scaffolding lock-in
Restructure the first card (slide 7) as a guided attempt rather than a revealed answer: show the card and the two categories, prompt with a reasoning question ('Think: do most students in Indonesia eat rice every day?'), and let the student drag first. Show the explanatory feedback ('Most students in Indonesia eat rice every day – this has a high chance') only AFTER the student places the card — correct or incorrect. This preserves the scaffolding intent while requiring an active first attempt, consistent with the IAS Explore stage.
Slide 7 provides a fully guided first card: the instruction tells the student exactly where to drag ('Drag the card into the HIGH chance category') and the feedback text is pre-displayed ('Most students in Indonesia eat rice every day – this has a high chance of happening'). This means the student receives the answer and reasoning before making any independent judgment. Slide 8 then shows the card already placed correctly. The scaffolding (answer + reasoning pre-revealed) persists through the first card without the student demonstrating any competence. From slide 9 onward the student works independently — so the lock-in is limited to the first card, but it does mean the first item is a passive observation rather than an active classification attempt.
⊘ Must fix✎edithigh confidence AP9 Action without reasoning
Add a persistent scaffolding prompt beneath the action instruction on all independent practice frames (slides 9 onward): 'Think: how often does this actually happen?' This one-sentence reasoning anchor directs students toward frequency-based thinking (addressing M280) without giving away the answer. The prompt should appear before the student drags, not only in error feedback.
From slide 9 onward, the instruction is 'Drag the card into the correct category' with no scaffolding text to guide what the student should think about when evaluating each new card. The only reasoning prompt appears in the incorrect-placement feedback (slide 10: 'Think about how often this happens'). For the remaining 8 cards after the worked example, there is no pre-action reasoning scaffold — students are told what to do (drag) but not what to think about (e.g., 'How often does this happen in real life?'). This is particularly important for M280 (the target misconception): students who treat likelihood as a vague feeling rather than a frequency judgment need a consistent reasoning prompt to build the habit.
+2
↑ Should fix P2 P · 3/5 +inserthigh confidence
After the first-card guided example (current slide 7–8 sequence), INSERT a Notice screen that shows the first two contrasting cards side by side (rice today vs teacher cancels all classes) with a brief prompt: 'Both are uncertain — but are they equally likely? What do you notice?' This directly surfaces M281.
+2
↑ Should fix P3 P · 2/4 +inserthigh confidence
On the Notice screen (to be inserted per P2 recommendation), add qualitative frequency bars beneath each contrasting card pair — a tall green bar for 'rice today' and a very short orange bar for 'teacher cancels all classes' — to introduce a pictorial representation of relative frequency without numbers.
+2
↑ Should fix P6 P · 2/4 +inserthigh confidence
Insert an Articulation screen (blueprint Stage 4) after the Notice screen.
+2
↑ Should fix D4 D · 2/4 +inserthigh confidence
Insert two screens between the guided first-card sequence and the full sort: (1) A Notice screen showing the rice card and the teacher-cancels card side by side in their correct zones with a prompt: 'Both are uncertain — but are they equally likely? What do you notice?' (no student input required, tap to continue).
+2
↑ Should fix D7 D · 2/4 ✎edithigh confidence
Two targeted edits: (1) EDIT the Launch screen (slide 1) to add a brief prerequisite activation: reference the Bogor rain vs helicopter contrast from the parent module ('Remember the two events we looked at — rain in Bogor and a helicopter at school? Let's sort more events like these.') and include the HIGH/LOW framing note ('HIGH and LOW are a starting point — chance is a spectrum from impossible to certain').
+1
◦ Consider P5 P · 2/3 ✎edithigh confidence
Confirm that the 10-card set includes at least 2 borderline cases — events that are genuinely HIGH or LOW but require reasoning rather than extreme-case recognition.
+1
◦ Consider D1 D · 3/4 +inserthigh confidence
Insert a Launch observe-screen before slide 6 that shows the Bogor rain vs helicopter contrast (two images with bold question marks) and the framing caption: 'Both of these could happen tomorrow — but do they have the SAME chance? We are sorting into HIGH and LOW to practise comparing chance — but remember, chance is a spectrum from impossible to certain.' This is the Stage 1 screen the blueprint specifies and the convention requirement for HIGH/LOW framing.
+1
◦ Consider D2 D · 3/4 ✎edithigh confidence
After the guided first card (rice), add 1–2 intermediate cards presented without pre-reveal but with the frequency-reasoning helper banner still visible ('Think: does this happen most of the time, or almost never?').

◦ Consider D3 D · 2/3 ✂splithigh confidence
Split slide 11 into two screens: (a) a 'Sort complete' confirmation screen showing all 8 cards in their zones with no feedback text — student reviews their work; (b) a Consolidate screen showing the contrastive pair reveal (Bogor rain vs helicopter spotlighted) with the closing explanation and the 'Conclude' CTA.
+1
◦ Consider D5 D · 2/3 +inserthigh confidence
Add a mid-sort segue screen after the second card is correctly placed: show the two sorted cards side by side with the prompt 'Both are uncertain — but are they equally likely?' before advancing to the full sort.

◦ Consider X1 X · 3/4 +inserthigh confidence
After the first worked card (rice today — slide 7/8 transition), INSERT a Notice screen that places the rice card and the 'teacher cancels all classes' card side by side in their correct zones with a brief qualitative frequency contrast ('Both are uncertain — but one happens almost every day, the other almost never').

◦ Consider X2 X · 3/4 ✎edithigh confidence
On slide 10 (incorrect feedback frame), add a second feedback variant for borderline cards (e.g., 'At least one student absent this month' or 'Someone will yawn during a lesson'): 'This one is tricky! Think about how often this happens for most students your age.' This acknowledges partial correctness difficulty and matches the research_brief's M283 surfacing strategy.

◦ Consider X4 X · 2/3 ✎edithigh confidence
On slide 6 (the primary interactive screen), add a dev note specifying the drag-state animation: 'When a card is lifted, enlarge it to 1.1× with a drop shadow.

◦ Consider X5 X · 2/3 ✎edithigh confidence
On slide 6 (initial card reveal state), add an orange accent border or drag-handle icon to the event card to signal it is draggable — consistent with IAS orange-accent convention for interactive elements.

◦ Consider X6 X · 3/4 ✎edithigh confidence
On slide 8 (transition to independent practice), replace the scaffolding text 'Now it's your turn — drag each card to the right category' with a reasoning prompt: 'Think: does this happen most of the time, or almost never?' This shifts the scaffolding from action-guidance to reasoning-guidance, consistent with the blueprint's helper banner and the IAS scaffolding principle that scaffolding text should guide reasoning, not just action.

◦ Consider L1 L · 3/4 ✎edithigh confidence
On slide 1 (Launch), change 'Let's look at some uncertain events' to 'Let's look at some uncertain outcomes — situations where we cannot be sure what will happen.' On slide 12 (completion summary), add one line: 'These are all uncertain outcomes — we cannot predict them with certainty.' This delivers 'uncertain outcome' as a compound term in context, satisfying the vocabulary_status requirement without re-vaulting the definition.
