Content role not fulfilled.
Interaction progression incomplete.
Recommended Enhancements (4) — advisory, not verdict-affecting

These are placement suggestions from advisory gates — enrichments that would strengthen the module but do NOT fail it. Each entry targets a specific slide position with a specific trope/stage addition, grounded in the blueprint's applet sandwich and seven-trope conventions.

G2 ·
Dedicated Notice/Contrastive stage screen — Between Slide 10 (incorrect-placement feedback) and Slide 11 (full sort / Conclude screen) — insert as a new observe-mode screen after the second card is correctly placed
Why: The blueprint's Stage 3 (Notice) requires a side-by-side contrastive panel spotlighting the two anchor cards (rice for lunch vs teacher cancelling all classes) with qualitative frequency bars, directly targeting M281 (equally-likely default). Without this screen, the contrastive insight is buried in per-card feedback text rather than made explicit as a pattern-recognition moment.
How: Show the two most contrasting sorted cards side by side in their correct zones with a spotlight effect; add a comparison caption: 'Both are uncertain — but do they have the SAME chance?' and a 'I see the difference →' tap-to-continue button before the student sorts the remaining cards.
G2 ·
Explicit Articulation/Explain stage (sentence-completion or oral prompt) — After the contrastive Notice screen (new slide, before the independent sort of remaining cards)
Why: The blueprint's Stage 4 (Explain) requires students to generate the principle in their own words via a sentence-completion panel ('Both events are [uncertain] — but they do NOT have the [same] chance, because one happens [much more often] than the other'). This activates the Shout_Out_Loud trope moment and directly addresses M281 at the articulation level. Currently the applet moves from card-level feedback directly to the full sort without an articulation checkpoint.
How: Insert a tap-to-select sentence-completion screen: 'Both events are ___ — but they do NOT have the ___ chance, because one happens ___ than the other.' Three blanks, 2–3 word choices each. After correct completion, show 'Say it aloud to a partner!' prompt.
G2 ·
Framing note at Launch acknowledging HIGH/LOW as scaffold (not full spectrum) — Slide 1 (Launch screen) — add a sub-caption below the sort instruction
Why: Blueprint convention requirement: the Launch screen must include 'We are sorting into HIGH and LOW to practise comparing chance — but remember, chance is a spectrum from impossible to certain. HIGH and LOW are not the only options!' Without this, students may leave with a binary misconception contradicting the parent module's chance strip.
How: Add a small italicised note below the 'Tap Start to begin' button: 'Note: HIGH and LOW are a starting point — chance is actually a full spectrum from impossible to certain!'
G2 ·
Closing framing note at Consolidate bridging to M2's chance line — Slide 12 (Activity Completed screen) — add a forward-bridge sentence
Why: Blueprint convention requirement: the Consolidate screen must acknowledge that HIGH/LOW is a scaffold and preview M2's chance line to prevent false-precision misconception. Currently Slide 12 states the key principle but does not bridge forward.
How: Add below the key principle: 'In the next lesson, you will place events more precisely on a chance line — HIGH and LOW are just the beginning!'
Translation Notes (13) — localisation metadata, not scored

The scorers encountered these bilingual term references in the curriculum context and blueprint. They are preserved here as deliverables for a later translation or localisation pass. The native-language fragments were NOT used to score vocabulary coverage — only the primary term was matched against delivered slide content.

Primary Native Lang Slide Type Context
chance kemungkinan Indonesian — vocabulary The informal Indonesian term for likelihood/chance used in everyday speech; the applet uses 'chance' in English delivery
uncertain outcome kejadian tidak pasti Indonesian — vocabulary A result that cannot be predicted with complete confidence before the event occurs; the applet's framing text reinforces
certain pasti Indonesian — vocabulary Upper boundary of the chance spectrum — an event that always happens without exception. Referenced in the applet's frami
impossible mustahil Indonesian — vocabulary Lower boundary of the chance spectrum — an event that can never happen. Referenced in the applet's framing note.
probability peluang Indonesian — vocabulary Primary Indonesian term for probability used in Kurikulum Merdeka textbooks; deliberately excluded from this applet — 'c
probability kemungkinan Indonesian — vocabulary Informal Indonesian term for likelihood/chance; used in everyday speech. This applet uses 'chance' in English delivery t
sample space ruang sampel Indonesian — vocabulary Out of scope for this applet — introduced in M3. Carried forward for downstream consumers.
event kejadian Indonesian — vocabulary Out of scope for this applet — introduced in M3. Carried forward for downstream consumers.
outcome hasil Indonesian — vocabulary Out of scope for this applet — introduced in M3. Carried forward for downstream consumers.
P(A) P(kejadian A) Indonesian — notation Out of scope for this applet — notation introduced in M8. Carried forward for downstream consumers.
S (sample space) S (ruang sampel) Indonesian — notation Out of scope for this applet. Carried forward for downstream consumers.
HIGH Chance Kemungkinan TINGGI Indonesian — vocabulary The label for the high-likelihood drop zone in the applet; the Indonesian equivalent for localisation reference. Student
LOW Chance Kemungkinan RENDAH Indonesian — vocabulary The label for the low-likelihood drop zone in the applet; the Indonesian equivalent for localisation reference. Student-
Research Brief
Learning Objective
Sort uncertain events into 'HIGH Chance' or 'LOW Chance' categories by dragging event cards, demonstrating that chance varies across situations.
Misconceptions (3)
#280 — Students treat chance as a vague linguistic label ('probably', 'maybe', 'unlikely') and resist comparing uncertain events with any precision. In this applet's binary-sort context, the risk is that students drag cards arbitrarily — treating HIGH and LOW as interchangeable labels — rather than reasoning about which situations genuinely have more chance. The applet surfaces this when students place events like 'rain in Bogor in January' in LOW Chance or 'helicopter landing at school tomorrow' in HIGH Chance without any reasoning anchor.
Design the feedback flow so that when a card is placed incorrectly, the system does NOT simply say 'wrong' — instead it prompts: 'Think about how often this usually happens. Does it happen most of the time, or almost never?' This forces the student to reason from frequency rather than from vague feeling. The Repair-Guided Feedback archetype (IAS id 34) is the correct vehicle.
Severity: medium none IAS: Repair-Guided Feedback
#281 — Students believe all uncertain events are equally likely — if something can happen or not happen, they default to 50/50. In a binary HIGH/LOW sort, this manifests as students placing all uncertain events in the same category (typically HIGH, since 'it could happen') or expressing that the sort is arbitrary because 'both could happen'. This is the highest-severity misconception for this applet and the primary reason the binary sort interaction was designed.
Include at least two event-card pairs in the sort set where one event is clearly HIGH and the other clearly LOW, drawn from the same domain (e.g., 'It will rain in Bogor tomorrow in January' vs 'A helicopter will land at your school tomorrow'). When a student places both in the same category, the feedback should contrast the two: 'Both are uncertain — but are they equally likely? Think about how often each one actually happens.' The Contrastive Feedback archetype (IAS id 32) is the correct vehicle for this pair-level feedback.
Severity: high none IAS: Contrastive Feedback
#283 — Students can identify impossible and certain events but treat all uncertain events as vague and undifferentiated — they cannot reason about relative likelihood between uncertain events. In the binary sort, this surfaces as random placement of uncertain events, or as students correctly placing the extreme cases (if any appear) but failing on the middle-range events. The applet's binary sort is a simplified version of the full ordering task — it reduces the cognitive demand to a two-category decision, which is the appropriate scaffold at this concrete phase.
Include 2–3 event cards that are 'borderline' (e.g., 'You will eat rice for lunch today' — HIGH for most Indonesian students; 'You will score 100% on your next test' — LOW but not impossible). These borderline cards force students to reason rather than rely on extreme-case recognition. Feedback for borderline cards should acknowledge the reasoning challenge: 'This one is tricky! Think about how often this happens for most students your age.' The Repair-Guided Feedback archetype (IAS id 34) handles this.
Severity: medium none IAS: Repair-Guided Feedback
Visual Models (1)
VIR 3 — Binary Chance Sort — Two-Zone Drop Area (HIGH Chance / LOW Chance)
The applet's primary visual structure: two clearly labelled drop zones (HIGH Chance in green, LOW Chance in orange/red) with a stack of draggable event cards. Students drag each card to the zone they judge correct. This is a simplified, two-category instantiation of the qualitative chance strip introduced in the parent module (VIR 3 precursor). The binary structure reduces cognitive load compared to the full ordering task (M2's chance line) while still requiring students to discriminate between uncertain events — directly targeting M281
Misconception high
Students believe all uncertain events are equally likely — if something can happen or not happen, they default to 50/50. In a binary HIGH/LOW sort, this manifests as students placing all uncertain events in the same category (typically HIGH, since 'it could happen') or expressing that the sort is arbitrary because 'both could happen'. This is the highest-severity misconception for this applet and the primary reason the binary sort interaction was designed.
Include at least two event-card pairs in the sort set where one event is clearly HIGH and the other clearly LOW, drawn from the same domain (e.g., 'It will rain in Bogor tomorrow in January' vs 'A helicopter will land at your school tomorrow'). When a student places both in the same category, the feedback should contrast the two: 'Both are uncertain — but are they equally likely? Think about how often each one actually happens.' The Contrastive Feedback archetype (IAS id 32) is the correct vehicle for this pair-level feedback.
 (equally-likely default). The colour coding (green = HIGH, orange = LOW) should be consistent with the parent module's Slide 9 reveal (high chance in green, low chance in orange).
concrete consolidate moderate
IAS Patterns (4)
Constrained Drag — Card-to-Zone Sort
Actions: Students pick up an event card and drag it to either the HIGH Chance or LOW Chance drop zone. Cards snap into the target zone on release; invalid drops (outside both zones) return the card to the stack. The dragging_vs_resting visual state (IAS id 49) governs the drag affordance: card enlarges to 1.1x, gains shadow, and the valid drop zones pulse with a highlighted border when the card is in flight. This is the primary interaction mechanic for the entire applet.
When: For every event card in the sort set — this is the core interaction loop. Used throughout the applet from first card to last.
Repair-Guided Feedback — Frequency Prompt
Actions: When a card is placed in the wrong zone, the system does not simply mark it incorrect. Instead, it displays a targeted prompt tied to the specific event: 'Think about how often this usually happens — does it happen most of the time, or almost never?' The card is returned to the stack (or held in the zone with an orange incorrect indicator) and the student must re-place it. This directly addresses M280 (vague linguistic labelling) and M283 (inability to reason between extremes) by anchoring the correction to frequency reasoning rather than abstract correctness.
When: On every incorrect placement. The prompt text is event-specific (pre-authored for each card in the set) to maximise relevance. Do not use generic 'Try again' feedback.
Contrastive Feedback — Pair Reveal
Actions: After the student has sorted all cards, the applet reveals a side-by-side comparison of the two most contrasting card pair (e.g., 'Rain in Bogor in January' vs 'Helicopter landing at school tomorrow') with their correct zones highlighted and a brief explanation: 'Both are uncertain — but rain in Bogor in January happens very often, while a helicopter landing at school almost never does. That is why their chance is different.' This directly targets M281 (equally-likely default) at the consolidation stage.
When: At the end of the sort, after all cards have been placed and the student has received individual card feedback. This is the applet's closing consolidation moment before the parent module's Exit_Question.
Highlight / Pulse / Emphasis — Drop Zone Activation
Actions: When a card is being dragged, both drop zones pulse with a coloured border (green pulse for HIGH Chance zone, orange pulse for LOW Chance zone) to signal valid drop targets. This reduces the cognitive overhead of remembering where to drop and keeps attention on the reasoning task (which zone?) rather than the motor task (where is the zone?).
When: During every drag operation — triggered by card pickup, deactivated on card release.
Convention Flags (4)
chance vs probability — applet feedback strings
All applet feedback strings must use 'chance' exclusively. Review every feedback string before publication. Acceptable: 'This event has a HIGH chance of happening.' Not acceptable: 'The probability of this event is high.' This applies to correct feedback, incorrect feedback, and the closing contrastive reveal.
Risk: Applet feedback strings are the most likely place for 'probability' to leak into student-facing content. Auto-generated or templated feedback may default to 'the probability of this event is high' rather than 'this event has a high chance of happening'. This would undermine the module's deliberate deferral of the formal term to M8.
HIGH / LOW as binary labels — risk of false precision
Include a brief framing note at the start of the applet (or as a closing summary): 'We are sorting into HIGH and LOW to practise comparing chance — but remember, chance is a spectrum from impossible to certain. HIGH and LOW are not the only options!' This bridges back to the parent module's Slide 12 chance strip and forward to M2's chance line.
Risk: Students may leave the applet believing that all events are either HIGH or LOW with no middle ground, contradicting the chance strip's continuous spectrum established in the parent module. The binary sort is a scaffold, not the final model — but if the applet does not acknowledge the simplification, it may create a new misconception.
Indonesian event card contexts
All event cards must use Indonesian everyday contexts. Recommended card set: (1) 'It will rain in Bogor tomorrow in January' — HIGH; (2) 'A helicopter will land at your school tomorrow' — LOW; (3) 'You will eat rice for lunch today' — HIGH; (4) 'You will score 100% on your next maths test' — LOW; (5) 'At least one student in your class will be absent this week' — HIGH; (6) 'You will find a 50,000 rupiah note on the ground today' — LOW; (7) 'The sun will rise tomorrow morning' — note: this is CERTAIN, not HIGH — use with care or replace; (8) 'Your school will be visited by the President tomorrow' — LOW. Avoid snow, lottery tickets, and other non-Indonesian contexts.
Risk: If event cards use generic Western contexts (e.g., 'It will snow tomorrow', 'You will win the lottery'), they will be culturally inauthentic and may confuse Indonesian students who have no experiential reference for snow probability.
Colour coding consistency with parent module
Use green for the HIGH Chance drop zone and orange/amber for the LOW Chance drop zone, matching the colour coding established in the parent module's Slide 9 reveal. Do not use red for LOW Chance — red carries a punitive connotation (IAS id 42 convention: incorrect state uses orange/yellow, not red).
Risk: If the applet uses different colours for HIGH/LOW than the parent module used for high/low chance labels (Slide 9: green = high, orange = low), students will experience visual discontinuity that increases cognitive load.
Research Gaps (3)
visual_model: No KB VIR exists for a binary two-zone card sort at the concrete/introductory probability level. VIR 3 (Probability Number Line) is used as the closest proxy, but the actual visual model — two labelled drop zones with draggable event cards — is a gap in the KB. The applet design must be built from scratch without a KB-grounded visual model template.
The blueprint builder must design the two-zone sort layout without KB VIR grounding. Flag for KB insertion as a new VIR: 'Binary Chance Sort — Two-Zone Drop Area', concrete phase, probability_development domain, grade range [6,9).
interactive: No KB IAS pattern exists specifically for a probability card-sort interaction at the concrete phase. The Constrained Drag archetype (IAS id 16) provides the drag mechanic, and Repair-Guided Feedback (IAS id 34) and Contrastive Feedback (IAS id 32) provide the feedback archetypes, but no integrated 'card sort with misconception-targeted feedback' pattern exists in the KB for this use case.
The blueprint builder must compose the interaction from multiple IAS archetypes (Constrained Drag + Repair-Guided Feedback + Contrastive Feedback) without a single KB-grounded pattern for the full interaction. This is a gap-fill design task.
misconception: The KB misconception records for M280, M281, and M283 remain misaligned with the introductory qualitative level of this applet (as noted in module research). M281 in the KB describes adding probabilities for compound events — not the equally-likely default for uncertain events at the qualitative sorting level. The applet-level misconception descriptions above are inferred from curriculum context and module research, not directly grounded in KB records.
Downstream scorers should note that KB M280/M281/M283 IDs are proxies. The actual misconceptions addressed are qualitative precursors. No new KB IDs are available — the gap is carried forward from module research.
Synthesis Narrative
This applet is the primary interactive vehicle for M281 — the equally-likely default misconception — and its design logic flows directly from the productive conflict established in the parent module's Slides 8–9 (Bogor rain vs helicopter contrast). Where the parent module showed students that two uncertain events can have different amounts of chance, this applet requires students to act on that insight: they must sort a set of uncertain event cards into HIGH Chance and LOW Chance zones by dragging, making the discrimination active rather than passive. The binary sort is a deliberate scaffold — it reduces the ordering task from a continuous spectrum (which belongs to M2's chance line) to a two-category decision, keeping cognitive load appropriate for the concrete CPA phase while still forcing students to reason about relative likelihood rather than treating all uncertain events as equivalent. The three misconceptions converge on a single interaction design principle: feedback must be frequency-anchored, not correctness-labelled. M280 (vague linguistic labelling) is addressed by Repair-Guided Feedback that asks 'how often does this usually happen?' rather than simply marking the placement wrong. M281 (equally-likely default) is addressed by the Contrastive Feedback pair reveal at the end of the sort, which places the two most contrasting cards side by side and explicitly names the difference in frequency. M283 (inability to reason between extremes) is addressed by including 2–3 borderline cards in the set — events that are genuinely HIGH or LOW but require reasoning rather than extreme-case recognition. The visual state requirements (IAS ids 41, 42, 49) govern the drag affordance and feedback colours: green for HIGH Chance, orange for LOW Chance, consistent with the parent module's Slide 9 colour coding. The applet's scope discipline is tight: it does not introduce the chance line (M2), does not use numerical probability (M8), and does not introduce the terms 'more likely', 'less likely', or 'equally likely' (M2 vocabulary). Its sole job is to consolidate the binary discrimination between HIGH and LOW chance using Indonesian everyday contexts, surface M281 through the contrastive pair reveal, and prepare students for the Exit_Question in the parent module's final slide. The parent module should ensure the Exit_Question (Pak Budi's two bags) follows this applet directly — the applet's closing contrastive reveal is the conceptual bridge to that diagnostic question. No Math_Trap trope belongs inside this applet; if a Math_Trap is warranted for M281 at the qualitative level, it should be placed as a module-level slide before this applet, not within the applet's interaction flow.
Inheritance Notes
Module research confirmed: (1) The parent module (G9C4M1) established 'chance' via Math_Vault (Slide 7) and introduced the qualitative chance strip concept via Slide 12's four-position spectrum — this applet consolidates both. (2) The Bogor rain vs helicopter contrast (Slides 8–9) is the productive-conflict anchor that this applet extends into active sorting — students have already seen the contrast; now they must apply the reasoning to new events. (3) The module research identified 'uncertain outcome' as a vocabulary gap (not_introduced in the knowledge_trace) — this applet should reinforce the concept in framing text without re-vaulting. (4) The false-friend risk for 'probability' (KB id 155) is inherited and active — all applet feedback strings must use 'chance' exclusively. (5) The module research confirmed that M281's structured surfacing (Two-Bag Contrast applet) was absent from the delivered module — this applet (G9C4M1A2) is the primary vehicle for that surfacing, making it a gap-fill for the module's missing interaction. (6) The sibling applet G9C4M1A1 (Three-Category Sort) handled certain/impossible/uncertain classification — this applet assumes that classification is established and focuses exclusively on discriminating within the uncertain category.
Ideal Blueprint
Blueprint LO
Sort uncertain events into 'HIGH Chance' or 'LOW Chance' categories by dragging event cards, demonstrating that chance varies across situations.

How to read this review

A review is the scorer's diagnosis of a single module (or applet). The per-slide grid at the bottom is the main working area; the sections above it are summaries and context.

Header & scores

  • Verdict (ship / upgrade / rework / fail) and overall score summarise the compiled review.
  • Mastery coverage is the S8 estimate of how much of the LO the module actually delivers.
  • Reviewer directives banner (amber, near the top) lists any approved-gate directives that flowed into the scorers this run.
  • Quadrant strip shows P / D / X / L percent scores with S9 blurbs.

Vocabulary Registry

A module-level table of every term this module is responsible for: A1's planned terms (with authoritative definitions), KB-supplementary terms, and scorer-discovered terms. Each row shows delivery status (established / mentioned / not introduced) and source. This is the one-glance answer to "what vocabulary does this module own?"

What's Working Well

Positive observations surfaced from scorer reasoning — criteria that scored at max, grouped by quadrant. The full reasoning text is included so you can see what the scorer thought was strong and why. Previously these stayed buried inside the collapsed Scorer details; this card pulls them up front.

Gates & Antipatterns

Module-level scoring signals. Gates are hard/critical pass/fail checks; antipatterns are structural issues found by S7. Click any failed gate or antipattern id for a description in the modal.

Slide Review (per-slide grid)

Each slide row has the thumbnail, the scorer-synthesised slide action wrapped in a priority chip (see symbol legend below), and a collapsed "Scorer details" block with the full criterion annotations. Below that sits the enrichment strip — each row is a distinct commitment the blueprint or scorer anchored to this slide:

  • Concept — what the reviewer actually saw (or thinks should be added) on this slide. Chips are tagged by action: introduces (green — slide establishes the concept for the first time in the module), reinforces / practices (blue — slide revisits a concept established earlier), add here (red dashed — reviewer recommends the slide deliver this concept but it currently does not), reinforce harder (amber dashed — slide mentions the concept but the reviewer recommends exercising it harder). Each chip carries a strength tag (explicit / partial / weak / missing) and an evidence snippet in the tooltip. Sourced from the S6 scorer's concepts_on_slide array (P and D quadrants) — what the reviewer observed, not what the blueprint planned.
  • Vocab introduced — A1-planned terms the module introduces at this slide, from blueprint.vocabulary_introduction_plan[].introduced_at_slide. Chip tooltip shows the authoritative definition.
  • Vocab in screen (applet reviews only) — terms the screen exercises. Sub-tag reinforced means the screen actively practices the term (drag / tap / input / generate); used means the term only appears passively.
  • Misc addressed — misconception ids the slide explicitly targets, with effectiveness 0/3. Primary source is blueprint.slides[].misconceptions_targeted (plan); the scorer's evidence_slides feeds the effectiveness score. Click the chip for the full how-addressed narrative.
  • Visual model — VIR id + title + CPA phase used on this slide. Click to open the VIR's full SVG preview and KB metadata in a modal.
  • Prerequisites — prior-module / prior-chapter concepts this slide reactivates. Populated on Activate-section bridges. From blueprint.slides[].prerequisites_activated.
  • Convention — false-friend or convention requirements the slide must satisfy. From blueprint.convention_requirements[].addressed_at_slide.
  • Textbook — textbook pages the slide uses, with usage tag (use_as_is / adapted / supplemented / referenced). From blueprint.textbook_alignment[].slide_positions.
  • Exit — marker on the slide that is the module's exit-verification question (from blueprint.trope_map.exit_question).
  • Flagged — criterion ids the scorer rated below max on this slide. Use this to jump directly to the problem spots.
  • Stage (applet reviews only) — 1-6 stage tag + stage name + cognitive action + interaction mode + scaffolding level + CPA phase, derived from the applet blueprint screen that this PPTX slide belongs to.

Click any chip with a tooltip indicator to open its full detail in a modal.

Priority & operation symbols

The Path to 100 section and each slide's action card use pill chips instead of inline text. Scan by glyph:

  • ⊘ Must fix — gate or antipattern requires this before ship
  • ↑ Should fix — quality upgrade recommended
  • ◦ Consider — nice-to-have, not blocking

Operation chips next to each action name the recommendation verb from the recommendation protocol:

  • ✖delete remove slide / content
  • +insert add new slide / element
  • ✎edit modify existing content
  • ↵merge combine two into one
  • ✂split one slide into several
  • ⇄relocate move to different position — notably for worked-example → applet conversion

Mastery Assessment

S8's narrative assessment: coverage percent with notes, what students CAN do after the module, fluency gaps, conceptual depth, transfer readiness, and the mastery path to 100. Per-misconception detail now lives in each slide's enrichment strip instead of a separate list here.

Path to 100

Priority-ordered upgrade actions from the scorer's upgrade_map, grouped as:

  • Gate recommendations — must-fix items from failed hard/critical gates or antipatterns. Lead with a priority chip + operation chip + source label.
  • Priority actions — per-criterion upgrades sorted by gap magnitude. Priority is derived from the gap (gap ≥ 3 → must-fix, ≥ 2 → should-fix, otherwise consider) when the scorer didn't emit one explicitly. Each row links back to the criterion's annotation anchor in the slide grid.
  • Mastery gaps — narrative gaps from S8 that don't have a specific criterion owner.

Research brief & Blueprint overlays

The linked Research brief and Blueprint overlays contain the upstream inputs the scorers used — misconceptions, VIRs, IAS patterns, slide sequence, vocabulary introduction plan, textbook alignment. Open them from the overlay buttons to cross-reference with the review.
